item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Added to the Esoteric Classes Guide under "No Class Listed"; originally held off because I worried it might dip into the negatives. Cool to see it still up!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You knocked this one out of the park

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What an idiot he was, and I was an even bigger one for letting him go. There was a part of myself that knew it was a trap, but I didn't listen. It was an offer too good to pass up — I don't even remember what I saw.

Hey, so this paragraph, which you really need to land perfectly because it's the whole crux of the piece, is masterful. This POV turn would have been so easy to make either a) very on-the-nose or b) deeply confusing, and you avoid both gracefully.

Personally, I think the format screw is perfectly and fully justified by the in-article context (which in turn is actually a fairly crucial bit of the narrative architecture, hence why a tale wouldn't be appropriate).

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Y'know, this is something I really dig. It's hard to put my finger on why exactly this works but it does and I definitely agree that this needed to be a SCP to work. As a tale, it'd feel a little unsatisfying and out of place; but as a SCP it's the right level of "hey what the fuck is going on" to feel right.

+ minor things that when i started was one thing and then i accidentally them all oops
- y'know these are all arguably excusable in narrative too so like, no pressure to change them?

Dr. Kitty Kearns

Dr. Kitty is a very good name for a doctor thank you for blessing us with this.

So, we arrived.

I kinda feel like this sentence can be dropped completely?

Took a bit to put that bit together.

The repetition of "bit" here feels a little weird: I feel like the first could be swapped with "while" or perhaps "sec"/"minute"

going inside;

a flashlight;

I kinda feel like both of these should be : tho that might just be me liking : too much

He wanted to go as far into the pothole without entering.

this feels like it's missing an "as he could" somewhere (either before or after the "without entering")

He instructed we grab hold of his ankles and hoist him below.

that we should be a "us to"

To if he saw something: yes

This feels a little clunky; it's not like, grammatically incorrect but it feels a little off?

But what he saw?

"But what had he seen?"

Quinn Lake (the personnel ID attached to this SCP file)

I kinda feel like this should've been established near the start.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So then why do they even include it, if they have so little information? Either treat it as possibly real and write up a document that includes the total lack of any other available information and explains why it's being treated seriously anyway, stick it in "possible leads to follow up on," or archive it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really, really like the concept. But right now it feels like it leaves too many unanswered questions to be in the realm of a charming mystery rather than frustrating. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nothing about this is related to dissociative identity disorder in any way. The pothole changes things into other things. Brooks does remember himself. He just does not introduce the writing as if it was him because he wants to appear less suspicious, but begins to ramble near the end.

Items that go in are forgotten about as well as the pothole, and only the output is remembered.

Also if the Foundation went off of little information, the actual file would be incredibly short. In 3001, they have a lot of information about the SCP and a lot more given by scantron. Brooks' account cannot be easily transformed into a coherent report.

Idk, that's how I see it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know, man. I still don't really understand how this SCP even works. So it messes with memories, but I have no clue how it even works.

Does the pothole give dissociative identity disorder to people who enter it? Because Brooks doesn't seem to remember himself at the beginning. Does everyone automatically forget about the person once they go through the pothole? Does everyone forget about the pothole itself, too? Because if the foundation still has a "little" bit of information on it, why don't they just include Brooks' account as a log or something? They did a pretty similar thing with SCP-3001.

I'm going to leave a novote for now because I might be missing something, but this really just doesn't feel right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While I understand why this was made the way it is, I don't like how it's presented here. Even the in-universe explanation isn't enough to to convince me. Novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The anomaly itself is only remembered by Brooks. All the foundation would have to go off of is his description of it. This SCP is detailing the anomaly of the pothole from a first-person point of view by the man affected by it. I don't believe a normal article would be easy for the foundation to write because they have little information about the anomaly, and Brooks' account is all they have.

I felt that the story would be more impactful if told this way. Brooks posted it on the database because he desperately wanted people to remember him, and knew it would be seen there. That's the in-universe explanation. So in my opinion, the screw is completely justified, but I see how people have different views.

Thanks for sharing your thoughts. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While i love a good format screw as much as the next person, there needs to be justification for the screw other than aesthetics. Nothing about the anomaly suggests that it prevents a regular article from being written about it, this could just as easily been added as a note attached to the article. The main body of the scip could even suggest that research is still ongoing and that proper containment procedures hadn't been devised if you think it's more than the safe scip it appears to be, but I don't think the Foundation would have this in their database as is. Novote, unless I'm just missing something somehow.

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded: As soon as a read this I knew this discussion page would be filled with these "this is a tale" remarks. This most certainly isn't — there's an anomaly at play and an in-universe reason for the documentation to be this way. While this could work as a tale, it also 100% works as an SCP because this is what an SCP is: detailing the affects of an anomaly and the anomaly itself in the most effective way possible for said anomaly.

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Hi there mastermindartemis, thanks for leaving your thoughts.

This is an SCP article, it’s just not written in the typical Item #, Object Class, Containment Procedures, Description, and Addendum format. I wrote the article this way because I felt that the story would be more impactful from a first person perspective.

I considered writing it as a tale, but at the end of the day, that’s not how I wanted to tell the story, because putting it in an article opposed to a tale actually does matter to the narrative.

Thanks again for leaving your thoughts.

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded: This doesn't seem to be an actual SCP article. It is almost more like a tale than a SCP. The whole thing is just a stream of conscious. Just letting you know because I rarely downvote pages.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Just whipped this up last night. Got bored, and got this idea.

Thanks tremendously to Uncle Nicolini for fixing up the formatting and prose, it really helps tell the story I want to tell.

Thanks to Gee0765, LordofLaugh, DrAkimoto, yesiam, Creagan_Stark, and like a million other people I can't name off the top of my head for helping with crit. I should really log that. Bop me on the head in the comments if you helped.

I hope you enjoy. :)
